content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Suggest me the best REVENGE movie you have ever watched

**Score:** 59
**Body:** I have watched some revenge movies but none ever had a plot with a revenge satisfying enough uk. But then i watched OLDBOY....safe to say i loved it.Since then i have been looking for other revenge movies like that but haven't found any. So, please suggest me a movie embodies the saying"Revenge is a dish best served cold".i promise i'll go watch it.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Sting


2. If you liked Old Boy (2003) you should watch: 

Sympathy for Mr. Vengeance (2002)

Lady Vengeance (2005)
3. The first John Wick movie and The Punisher with Thomas Jane.
4. Blue Ruin (2013)
5. Lucky Number Slevin
6. Dead Mans Shoes. Paddy Considine is fucking magnificent as a soldier returning to his home town.
7. The Count of Monte Cristo 

Sleepers
8. Law Abiding Citizen or Memento
9. Mandy!
10. Man on Fire. Top of my list.

## 2. We watched Mrs Doubtfire and loved it.

**Score:** 27
**Body:** We're now looking for something as funny that also discusses issues of adult-life, as does Mrs Doubtfire. We're not looking for children comedy 😁 thanks !

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Bird Cage
2. Mr Mom
3. Uncle Buck
4. Good Morning Vietnam
5. Parenthood came out when I was a very young teenager. Since then I’ve watched it at different stages of my life and it hits different every single time. First time I saw it, I identified with Joaquin Phoenix, second time, Keanu, then Tom Hulce’s character, and so on and so on. The comedy holds up well too, no real dated or offensive jokes and everyone gives amazing performances in it.
6. Hunt for the Wilderpeople
7. Father of the Bride
8. Trains, Planes and Automobiles
9. Tootsie

## 3. Best classic fantasy in the vein of "Excalibur," "Conan" and "Willow"

**Score:** 23
**Body:** Hi there! I'm interested in classic swords-and-sorcery and I'm looking for absolute classics. I'd really appreciate your expertise and recommendations. Thanks so much! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ladyhawke
2. The Sword and the Sorcerer

Highlander

Beastmaster
3. Legend

The Golden Voyage of Sinbad

Sinbad and the eye of the tiger

Clash of the titans

Krull
4. Maybe take a look at *Dragonslayer* (1981), IMO still the best dragon ever put on film, with absolutely the best dragon name (*Vermithrax Pejorative*, translated as "The Wyrm of Thrace that makes things worse"), and quite possibly the grubbiest Disney film ever produced.
5. Red Sonja
6. Dragonslayer

Krull
7. The 13th Warrior,  and Legend
8. Legend starring Tom Cruise, directed by Ridley Scott is pretty underrated
9. Jason and the Argonauts

Clash of the Titans
10. Check out The Green Knight, the one with Sean Connery.

## 4. Movies that transport you to a happy place you didn’t know existed

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I’m in Minneapolis and it feels like a war zone. My nervous system has not slowed down in months. I need to feel safe, warm, happy and optimistic for two hours. Send help. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What's Up Doc? takes place in the screwball land of San Francisco in the '70s. We could all use a laugh, and this one is hysterical.
2. Goonies
3. Studio Ghibli films hit different when you need that kind of escape - My Neighbor Totoro or Kiki's Delivery Service are like visual comfort food
4. Hook with Robin Williams
or
Porco Rosso
5. Big Fish

Princess Bride

Ratatouille kinda
6. Plesantville! 

This is one of my cozy movies. It takes you back to simpler times, warm and fuzzy ending.
7. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty
8. Perfect Days dir Wim Wenders
9. Finding Nemo
10. I Heart Huckabees

## 5. Comedies similar to “Airplane!” and “Scary Movie” that aren’t parodies

**Score:** 19
**Body:** These types of movies feel like they’re set in an alternate reality, where absurd things can happen, like a live action cartoon. But what I don’t want is a spoof of a specific genre. So no “Not Another Teen Movie” or “Spaceballs”. 

“Pee Wee’s Big Adventure” and “Barb and Star Go to Vista Del Mar” have a similar feel, as does the show “30 Rock”. Any other movies come to mind? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. BASEketball, from Airplane’s David Zucker. The dumbest movie and everyone involved knows it. Underrated.
2. Dale and Tucker vs Evil
3. shaolin soccer

kung fu hustle
4. Wayne’s World and its sequel are great absurdist 4th wall breaking comedies that aren’t a parody of something else.

Also This is Spinal Tap is great, but you could say it’s a parody of band biopics.
5. Better Off Dead
6. **Hundreds of Beavers** is about as close to live action cartoon as it gets.
7. Arrested Development
8. * UHF
* Jonny Dangerously
* The Mask
* The Marx brother's movies
* Top Secret
9. Psycho Beach Party (2001). Has some elements that parody Gidget, etc, but really isn’t just a parody movie. It’s great.
10. But I'm A Cheerleader 



---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. I believe Jack Nicholson is the only actor in Hollywood history to have left the film industry as a top-tier star. Are there others like him?

**Score:** 1565

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Gene Hackman
  - Even his last movie was actually enjoyable and not terrible ( Welcome to Mooseport)
2. Paul Newman maybe?
  - Shocked Paul Newman isn’t getting more love.  He retired a working legend
  - His final scene in Road to Perdition (his last film) is electrifying
3. Was gonna say Gene Hackman but got beaten to it so how about Rick Moranis? He left to look after his family but he was a headliner at the time.
  - Rick Moranis is coming back though, in Spaceballs 2.
4. Christopher Plummer was nominated for an Oscar at 88 (_All the Money in the World_), more than fifty years after _The Sound of Music_
5. Greta Garbo.
6. Grace Kelly. 

## 2. Zombieland is one of the greatest movies in the zombie genre and I really enjoyed the humor and the overall tone of the film.

**Score:** 297
**Body:** Jesse and Woody were top notch in it it had good cameos and great rewatch value.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This movie reawakened my love of Metallica. That opening sequence set to "For Whom The Bell Tolls" is  God Tier.
2. I love Zombieland but Shaun Of The Dead is way better. It's all subjective, I'll watch both if they are on.
  - It's not hip hop, it's electo, you prick.
3. Is Double Tap good?
  - Not nearly as good as the first one but it’s brainless enough to be a good popcorn flick.
  - I thought it was a very fun follow up!
4. Such a great movie, love it to pieces and could see this once a month no problem. Also, it's one of those rare cases where the sequel didn't destroy the original work and is very entertaining as well. 
5. "Do you have any regrets?"

"Garfield, maybe."
6. I think about it every time I eat a Twinkie, and the Bill Murray scenes are the funniest things ever.
7. I had such a crush on Emma stone this era. This pic of her gives mila kunis vibes

Great movie though. Very fun and even more fun with friends

## 3. Is there a movie with more badass lines?

**Score:** 278
**Body:** Okay, my friend. It's off to the next life for you. I guarantee you, you won't be lonely.

I got all the time in the world. \*You\* don't, but I do.

Last wish? I wish you had more time

He'll deliver more justice in a weekend than ten years of your courts and tribunals.

Forgiveness is between them and God. It's my job to arrange the meeting.

 A man can be an artist... in anything, food, whatever. It depends on how good he is at it. Creasy's art is death. He's about to paint his masterpiece.

There is no such thing as tough. There is trained and untrained. Now which are you?

Mariana: He's protected better than our president.

Creasy: He's gonna need it.

A bullet always tells the truth.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|e5RclbFtYbZmM)
2. "Forgiveness is between them and God. It's my job to arrange the meeting."

  
That has to be the hardest line ever.
  - Creasy's art is death, and he’s about to paint his masterpiece
    - Only Christoper Walken could deliver that line so fucking perfectly
3. I wish you had more time.
  - “I got time. You don’t.”
4. Tombstone

“You’ll die first. Your friends might get me but not before I make your head into a canoe.”
5. Army of Darkness.
6. A lot of great ones already mentioned so I have to add Watchmen for the single most badass line in cinematic history.

![gif](giphy|l0Hlyab9gW16cYBBm|downsized)

Edit: pity the gif cuts out before the payoff
7. Arnie in Commando


"I lied".

## 4. Favourite found footage style film?

**Score:** 186
**Body:** I just watched Chronicle (2012) and I really enjoyed the found footage style because of how important it is to the story itself. He has a reason to start filming everything, people ask him about it all the time, and the acting is all very down to earth because they’re just regular people that know they’re being filmed.

It led me to the question of what are some of the best found footage style films. What’s your favourite?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's probably Chronicle, tbh.
2. Cloverfield (2008)
  - This is the answer. So good.
  - One of my all time favorite movie theatre experiences
3. .REC
4. As Above So Below

Blair Witch 

The Poughkeepsie Tapes
5. Project x was underrated
6. The Blair Witch Project
7. I wouldn’t call it my favorite , but troll Hunter (2010) was very entertaining.
8. Fuck a cop but End of Watch is tight

## 5. MOVIE ADDICT

**Score:** 120
**Body:** Has anyone ever watched a movie so often over a period of time that you grew to know every line of said movie …what is that move for you ?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The whole trilogy is great. The Worlds end is the weakest entry but Hot Fuzz is hilarious
  - The World's End is the weakest, but it was the most surprising. I was NOT expecting the movie to go in the direction it did.
    - It is the weakest, but like its predecessors it has great rewatch quality. I definitely enjoyed it more on the second viewing.
2. ![gif](giphy|189C6HfKhiK1a)
3. Them beating the bar owner with pool cues ON BEAT with Queen's Don't Stop Me Now is one of the funniest scenes in any movie, ever.
4. Yea boii (this is also that movie for me)
5. Some of Edgar Wright’s Filmography. Scott Pilgrim and Hot Fuzz to name a few.
6. This is a rare case of a flawless film. Masterpiece.
7. This is fried gold. Perfect film.
8. ![gif](giphy|4sEv1yZqYrzzi)

Commando

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The early Farrelly brothers were some sort of auteurs

**Score:** 61
**Body:** I've been rewatching the Farrellys' earlier films: Dumb & Dumber, Kingpin, There's Something About Mary and Me, Myself & Irene. 

Setting aside how amazing these films are, what strikes me is what an intense authorial voice these movies have. Which is amazing, considering we're talking about gross-out, unambitious sex comedies. 

And it's not just the brand of humor. Even though the humor is very singular. 

'Roy, can you get sick drinking piss' - Is the sort of joke that is very much a Farrelly joke. And its delivery is intensely Farrelly. 

But it goes beyond their dedication to shooting every scene as if it were the punchline to the greatest joke ever: a Farrellys movie has a cinematic style that is unmistakable. Watching Kingpin again, I was struck by these images that just catch the eye. From the reptile handler revenge biting the cobra to the close-up of a performing Jonathan Richman, these movies are filled with images that are striking. 

I have a lot of trouble analyzing their movies and pinpointing what it is they exactly are able to pull of so well. Just wanted to point out how much of a letdown their later movies have been. It really is true that the Farrelly at some point started to make the sort of lazy movies that Sandler and Chris Farley were known for, and that the Farrelly had make look so pathetic for years.

What do you guys think of this response I'm having to the Farrellys?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Love the cinematography in Dumb and Dumber. Might be a product of its time. Maybe just nostalgia for me but 35mm was knocking it out the park for me in the 90s. It didn’t have that rough texture that you saw in the 80s and the colors really popped. 

I keep seeing this meme that Seinfeld has Rohmer qualities at times. It’s a silly joke, but I get it. We lost that look once digital intermediates got better in the late 90s/early 00s. Things got too slick, which is a choice. 

Jim Carrey and Jeff Daniels had amazing chemistry. And it’s a shame the sequel isn’t good at all. Unfortunately, what pulls me out of the sequel is the way it was shot. There’s way too much coverage. If you stay to the credits, there’s a Zamboni sequence that’s one long master shot and I wish they shot the whole movie like that. That’s where you feel the emotion of these silly characters who are really great friends.
  - I like most people have seen Seinfeld on repeat for decades. 

I have started to really watch it closely the last few times and it is shot incredibly, and has an insane amount of homages to so many things.  

Am also a huge Lynch fan and love the constant playing with the Twin Peaks cast and other amazing casting. And obviously the Woody Allen similarities. 

It’s true art, not just comedy but the filmmaking too.
    - A big part of the reason Seinfeld looks so great is because it wasn't lit according to the prevailing sitcom lighting norms of the time. Quite a few of the crew hadn't actually worked a sitcom before, and they kinda just made it up as they went along. So instead of total coverage, bathed in light, we get more three-dimensional lighting with some darker areas. Basically I think they used less of the super bright overheads and more spot lighting. I don't know much about lighting, but this is from memory, from the bonus features years ago.

I agree, it looks wonderful. My all-time favorite show, currently watching through on 4K.
      - You have me thinking about the fucking red neon abomination outside of Kramer's window
  - I’m not convinced the Rohmer quality of Seinfeld has anything to do with being pre-digital or a lack of “slickness.” To my eyes the comparison is 90% about production design and costume design, specifically the use of color. This is a look the show grew into. If you watch the first season it looks totally different – way duller and grungier.
2. Loudermilk (produced by Peter) has some of that early Farrelly charm. And if you haven't read it, The Comedy Writer, again by Peter, gives a fun fictionalised insight into his movings in Hollywood.

I think what's good about those early ones is how easy it came. Everyone was pulling their weight for the same thing. As time goes on it becomes more laboured, trying to capture the same feeling but being too established. It's like they tried to write films with purpose, or vehicles for actors, rather than simply enjoying themselves. Shallow Hal seems to be the tipping point.
3. I’m from Boston and my car broke down in some desert town in California for a week in 1998. I had nothing to do so I went to the movies and saw Something About Mary. Could not believe the amount of jokes that were purely Massachusetts (and RI) based ( “Healy, you look pissa!” ) and immediately felt so homesick. 

Not saying this is evidence of them being auteurs, but it is evidence of a singular vision outside of the Hollywood norm.
  - That's really cool and not something I had noticed in my 10 plus watches.  Probably because I'm a west coast fool for life.
4. I think your your feeling that they are kind of auteurs is about right.  Typically the term means that the director is the primary "author" of a movie, rather than the studio or the screenwriter.  They have a distinctive visual style, and recurring themes that they revisit across films.   In common usage, it snobbishly has a sense of connection to high art and prestige.  
  
To a certain extent, the term isn't really needed for folks like the the Farrely's who are already writting and are authors--it doesn't really elevate them to simply to use the fancypants term "auteur"  At the end of the day the success of a comedy film fundamentally comes down to the writting and the performances--hand the script and cast to another competent director and you ought to get something good.  So one could argue that even trying to apply the term is to give into a snobbish desire to elevate something one happens to like.    
  
And yet, even if the term "auteur" is extra annoying to apply to the comedy half of poetics, I think your response is grounded in something useful to explore.   
  
I might compare the them to a couple of Hong Kong comedy directors:  
  
Jeffery Lau is the king of mo lei tau (nonsense comedy), he has a very distinctive visual style and a driving pace that makes the Farrelys look sedated.  He's also a writer-director.  Art house programmers have an easy time treating him like auteur, which might reasonably come down to his thematic obsessions and very distinctive visual style, but probably, less reasonably, comes from his close association with WKW and the way that their films are often in conversation.   
  
Clifton Ko basically reinvented the Lunar New Year comedy ensemble. In terms of thematic obsession If the early Farrellys films often focus on a pure-ish protagonist undergoing a humiliating journey for the sake of perfect woman,  Ko focuses on the family. His films almost always end in a restoration of family values and harmony, after chaotic slapstick.  Sometimes he writes, sometimes he is only credited as a director.  Similarly he also prioritizes the actors' chemistry and comedic timing over complex cinematography. 

Ko and the Farrellys seem kind of the same--they've made strong, beloved films that are watched again and again years later.  If one feels that it's hard to fit them into the "auteur" framework, its because the framework itself is limited and overvalued. 
5. The climactic bowling scenes in Kingpin remain big, engaging, and memorable years after I saw the film. Also, last time I saw it, many of the jokes had aged and I didn't find it nearly as funny as I did a decade prior. But the Bill Murray bowling stuff at the end was still pretty damn good.

## 2. Musical scenes that elevate the film?

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Thinking about my favorite movies, I've found a pattern that I never realized before: a lot of those movies have a musical scene that may be the best scene of the movie.

Examples: Blue Velvet (in dreams), Aftersun (Under Pressure), El Sur (pasodoble at Estrella's communion), Mulholland Drive (the whole no hay banda scene).

I'm not looking for musical films; I'm looking for movies with this peculiarity. Can you think of some examples?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Donnie darko.  Tears for fears introducing all the main characters through a music sequence but then later on using the bunnymen song that the movie is probably based on when he is hallucinating during the party and then the ending of the movie with the cover of mad world.  Run on sentences abound in this post.
2. Portrait of a Lady on Fire (bonfire scene + ending)

The Double Life of Véronique (Weronika‘s concert)

Band of Outsiders (dancing scene)

Climax (that long take) 

And - not a movie, but a series - the ending of S1 Ep2 from Babylon Berlin … reminds me a lot of that scene from Blue Velvet!
  - the bonfire scene in portrait is seriously haunting, it sticks with you. also that climax long take is insane.
3. How about the Just Dropped In dream sequence in The Big Lebowski
4. I think most of my favourite scenes in film are that mix of image and music. Absolutely Club Silencio from Mulholland is up there, almost all of 2001: A Space Odyssey wouldn’t work without the classical textures. 

A recent one would be the use of Under Pressure in Aftersun. That works because it’s a great song, but also the way the song is manipulated and degrades and morphs is kind of the auditory equivalent of the drama of the scene.
5. Sinners has got to be the most recent and obvious example. I'd love to just say "Sinners" but because of the dipshit moderator rule I have to say a load more shite that has no real value. I didn't even want to say the "Most recent and obvious" bit because it makes me sound a bit cunty. Hopefully I've said enough now and that you've enjoyed reading it. Let's find out if I pass.
  - Hahahahahahahahahahahahahahahaha this is a great answer. I still haven't seen it, so I should do it asap. I may invite the moderator so it can check it too. It makes sure that we lose our time writing stupid shit because, obviously,  a good answer can't be put into one or two words. Are we crazy? It's way better having to write loads of shit like I am! What a delight it's been
  - The entire point of the moderator is to push you to actually *discuss* the contribution, rather than just dropping the title of the movie.
6. Basically the whole soundtrack to "Jaws." But I especially like the scenes where they are putting out to sea and first fighting the shark. It wouldn't be the same without the music.
7. All of Movern Callar, but Some Velvet Morning during the supermarket scene and Dedicated to the One I Love at the very end have stuck with me since I first saw it.

The Take My Breath Away sequence in As Tears Go By.

Another one that enters my head from time to time is Waltz of the Flowers in World of Tomorrow Episode 2.

Everybody’s Talking in Midnight Cowboy.

Tiny Dancer in Almost Famous.

## 3. Trying to find student film featuring cat and monn

**Score:** 2
**Body:** I think it was around 2016 when I watched this Arthouse Film on ARTE.
I remember that the movie featured a cat and the moon on a painted background. There also were real people acting in a weird story.
I don't quite remember the plot so I really want to see it again, but cannot find it using common search engines..
Is there anyone out there who might have some suggestions?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Copied from AI to hit the text limit:

Primary Recommendation: "The Moon" (2011)

* **Creator:** Jake Armstrong
* **Institution:** School of Visual Arts (SVA)
* **Description:** This highly acclaimed student film features a surreal and beautifully animated sequence involving a cat-like creature and a celestial narrative. It gained significant traction on Vimeo and YouTube in the early 2010s for its unique art style and atmospheric storytelling.

## 4. Thoughts on my psychological horror short?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I made it for my high school film class. Any sort of review or advice would be absolutely amazing. I shot it with real actors for two eight hour days. It was grueling but I think it was worth it. I wrote entirely at school in free periods and shot it on a Blackmagic 4k with some Nikkor F mount lenses. Im proud of it as my first narrative short but was just wondering if other people would like to see it! (:

https://youtu.be/07wiOoiriZo?si=LyM13B1xRrG1b8oQ

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 5. I didn´t get Hamnet

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I dont say this to be pretentious or to be anoying. I was really bored watching and i want to know why other people like it or if there is more people like me.   
I usually dont get bored watching a movie but this one really make me yawn. My main problem is that everyone felt very distant. At least to me a good drama makes me understand the people behind it. But at the end i really could not connect with any of the main characters. Obviosly If my child died i would be sad, but my conection with these characters begins and ends with this. The child, even being the best character in the movie, didnt have a very developed relationship with either of parents and he was too little in the movie for me to feel his death. I didn´t understand exactly why these character care for each other and what every one of them represented for the other.

For example, Amour and Manchester by the sea at the end of the movie i could felt exactly what every character felt and how was the relationship with the other characters in a very understandble way.   
The ending apart from being very well acted didn´t make me feel anything. Why Shakespeare would write a play about revenge? The only relationship i saw between the play and his life was the concept of death. But nothing more. If the play was about grief i could understand why the ending was the way it was but the play and his life couldn´t be more random one from the other. The concept of the metanarrative was kinda clever but i wish it was better used in a way I could understand why Shakespeare wrote the play the way it wrote it and overall i could see the character of Shakespeare writing Hamlet. But the way the movie is I never see any passion comming from Shakespeare toward the art of storytelling.  
Overall i didn´t like the setting very much. I never could feel immersed in the period. And how the characters talk mixing modern and old english randomly didn´t help.  
btw sorry for the broken English.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I enjoyed it more than you, it was a really beautiful film that was strongly acted, from the leads to the child actors. I don’t need to get super invested into characters for a movie to still hit its point. But I do agree with not understanding the connection to the play and the death of Shakespeare’s son. I’m not super familiar with the play but even the portion they included in the film didn’t really draw a connection for me.
  - fair enough. maybe if i didn´t watch it on 720p i would have enjoyed the visuals more.
2. I think the issue is that you expected a movie about Shakespeare:s passion for the art of storytelling, and that is not the focus of this film, at all.

It's understandable you would expect that, with the trailers playing now that start off by mentioning Shakespeare's name and have lots of images of that character.

But really, this is a film based on a book about the wife.  In the book, Shakespeare is never named.  He's just called 'the husband'.  The film doesn't reveal his name until the last 10 minutes.   Before that it's just hints and context clues.  But puzzling out his identity is not the point.

But this is the story of Agnes; he woman thought to be a witch, the falconer, the herbal healer, the woman who defied her family to force a marriage with a lower class man, who defied her family in giving birth as she wished 
, who raised kids with a largely absent husband, who fought death, who was consumed by grief.  The entire second half of the novel is about  grief.  

For me this was a very weepy book.  The way the twins swapped places when playing dressup being an aspect of what led to Hamnet's death was  a haaaaard watch.

It's obviously fine you didn't like it, but you might enjoy it more on rewatch going in with different expectations.
  - I had no expectations and knew nothing about it and came out a mess. I thought it might be my least favourite of the few that I saw recently but I was way off.
  - Na i expected her being the mc after the start. I just didint conect with hee
3. For the film being titled "Hamnet", Hamnet was the least important aspect of the film to me. To me it's a look at a relationship and transcending the hardships, which includes a great tragedy.

We start with the vibrant relationship between Agnes and Will, and like a good number of relationships, the dimension changes as time goes by and when they become parents. Agnes is paralyzed by her vision and too afraid about losing a  child and Will dissatisfied with his career, that they lose track of each other. And this loss that should unite them, creates even more distance. It's heartbreaking. 

Neither could speak of their pain to each other, so Will found transcendence in his work . Instead of portraying a man going mad over the death of his son, it's a son driven mad over the loss of his father.  Seeing a young man portray their son (a genius decision to have Jacobi Jupe's brother Noah, play older Hamnet) gives Agnes some peace. She can see what would have been had he lived; she gets to see him in this light with everyone taking him in and it brings him to life again for that moment. I just think it's a beautiful film.


  - It is a good reading of the film but i couldnt see that way watching the movie. Maybe it's My problem idk
4. I absolutely loved the parts with the children but anything involving Agnes + Will left me cold. The Shakespeare text readings didn't quite work as well as I expected or some reason, and neither did the final scene with the ubiquitous Max Richter piece. I wish it leaned more into the children the way the book did, their scenes were so naturally compelling. Simple scenes of cooking or playing were my favorites.

I did find the whole 'she's a forest witch' thing a bit YA novel-esque, but I had that problem when I read the book too (actually in the book it was more relentless).

Also I thought the writing for Will's abusive father was very very ham fisted in the first half.

---
